children.text: The expansion of the Canal’s watershed is not only a technical form of water management. It reorganizes the land and the communities that inhabit it. In this process, rural territories are reclassified according to their potential contribution to the Canal's water supply.

Within these redefined basins, infrastructure ceases to appear only as physical construction and operates instead as a mode of territorial reading: rivers, hills, and settlements become legible as storage capacity, catchment zones, or sites of future intervention.

For communities living inside these watersheds, however, this reclassification is experienced as a suspension of certainty. Land is held in a condition of provisional existence, shaped by official announcements, technical studies, and projected water futures with material effects long before a single dam or reservoir is built. Drawing on historical and ethnographic research, Ashley Carse (2014) demonstrates how the Canal’s surrounding landscapes and communities have been continually reorganized into "natural infrastructure" to sustain global trade corridors, reshaping local ecologies and livelihoods.

In Digna's account, this experience is not abstract but lived. Reflecting on the aftermath of the 2006 revocation of Law 44, she describes how uncertainty persisted even when official conflict appeared resolved:

This cycle of anticipation is central to the Río Indio project. It was originally announced over two decades ago but it was legally revoked following sustained campesino mobilizations. Yet in the wake of climate-change induced droughts, that have crippled Canal transits, the state has resurrected and formally advanced the damming of Río Indio as of 2026.
Within this temporal cycle, Digna's testimony appears across both archival and contemporary moments:
The continuity of this temporal structure becomes visible when comparing
Familia
(2007) with contemporary recordings of Digna's testimony. In
Familia

(2007), Digna is introduced while selling raffle tickets to finance opposition to the referendum for the Canal expansion. Nearly two decades later, she describes continued organizing through ticket sales and community fundraising to sustain renewed resistance to the same project. Her testimony makes visible a form of territorial knowledge grounded in anticipation rather than resolution. Instead of describing infrastructure as a finished object, her account registers it as an ongoing reorganization of territory and everyday life across time.
In this sense, water governance in the Panama Canal watershed is not linear but recursive: development is repeatedly proposed, suspended and reactivated across time.
Recommended readings:
Carse, Ashley.
Beyond the Big Ditch: Politics, Ecology, and Infrastructure at the Panama Canal
. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press, 2014.
Carse, Ashley, and David Kneas. 2019. “Unbuilt and Unfinished: The Temporalities of Infrastructure.”
Environment and Society
. New York: Berghahn Journals. doi:10.3167/ares.2019.100102.
Chirú, Félix J. 2007. “La Coordinadora Campesina contra los Embalses, Panamá: 1999–2006. Aproximación a un análisis cultural.”
Diálogos Revista Electrónica de Historia
